ppt.title: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers
ppt.presentationTitle: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers
ppt.presentationGoal: This session introduces Class IX students to the expansion of the number system from rational numbers to include irrational numbers. Students will learn definitions, properties, and representations on the number line.

licensingNotes: Use internally generated images and in-app SVG diagrams only.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Prefer SVG diagrams for concept/process slides and use reusable web images for all picture-based visuals.

licenseChecklist: All visuals should be internally generated or rendered as in-app SVG diagrams.
Review generated visuals for classroom accuracy before export.
presentationWarnings: Do not extend beyond the taught session scope.
Replace placeholder asset URLs with final selected reusable assets before export if needed.

learningOutcomesCovered: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
Students can construct and locate √2 on the number line geometrically.

slides.slideTitle: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers
learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the session and set the learning purpose.
slides.studentTakeaway: Understand what today’s lesson is about.
bulletPoints: Mathematics | Class IX
Number System
45 minute session
onSlideText: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers
Number System
Mathematics | Class IX
speakerNotes: This session introduces Class IX students to the expansion of the number system from rational numbers to include irrational numbers. Students will learn definitions, properties, and representations on the number line.
To bridge the gap between Class VIII number systems and higher mathematics.
To resolve the 'gap' in numbers where √2 exists but cannot be written as p/q.
slides.visualPlan: A clean classroom hero visual introducing Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers.

svgDiagram.title: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers visual
instructions: If a sourced image is not precise enough, use a compact classroom diagram for Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers.
Prefer labelled structure/flow/comparison visuals only when they directly improve teaching clarity.
svgDiagram.svgCode:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="960" height="720" viewBox="0 0 960 720" role="img" aria-label="Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers">
  <rect width="960" height="720" fill="#F8FBFD"/>
  <rect x="60" y="52" width="840" height="90" rx="24" fill="#1D4E89"/>
  <text x="90" y="108" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-weight="700" font-size="34" fill="#FFFFFF">Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers</text>
  <rect x="150" y="190" width="660" height="90" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#1D4E89" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="480" y="245" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="30" font-weight="700" fill="#1D4E89">Real Numbers</text>
  <line x1="480" y1="280" x2="480" y2="360" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="6"/>
  <line x1="280" y1="360" x2="680" y2="360" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="6"/>
  <rect x="120" y="380" width="280" height="170" rx="24" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#1D4E89" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="260" y="435" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="28" font-weight="700" fill="#1D4E89">Rational</text>
  <text x="260" y="478" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Integers, fractions,</text>
  <text x="260" y="510" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">terminating decimals</text>
  <rect x="560" y="380" width="280" height="170" rx="24" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="700" y="435" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="28" font-weight="700" fill="#D97706">Irrational</text>
  <text x="700" y="478" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Roots like √2, π,</text>
  <text x="700" y="510" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">non-repeating decimals</text>
</svg>

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="960" height="720" viewBox="0 0 960 720" role="img" aria-label="What We Will Learn">
  <rect width="960" height="720" fill="#F8FBFD"/>
  <rect x="60" y="50" width="840" height="90" rx="24" fill="#1D4E89"/>
  <text x="90" y="106" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-weight="700" font-size="34" fill="#FFFFFF">What We Will Learn</text>
  <line x1="120" y1="390" x2="840" y2="390" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  <polygon points="840,390 816,378 816,402" fill="#1F2937"/>
  <polygon points="120,390 144,378 144,402" fill="#1F2937"/>
  <g font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937" text-anchor="middle">
    <line x1="180" y1="360" x2="180" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="180" y="450">-2</text>
    <line x1="300" y1="360" x2="300" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="300" y="450">-1</text>
    <line x1="420" y1="360" x2="420" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="420" y="450">0</text>
    <line x1="540" y1="360" x2="540" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="540" y="450">1</text>
    <line x1="660" y1="360" x2="660" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="660" y="450">2</text>
    <line x1="780" y1="360" x2="780" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="780" y="450">3</text>
  </g>
  <rect x="110" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#1D4E89" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="140" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#1D4E89">Rational Numbers</text>
  <text x="140" y="585" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Can be written as p/q</text>
  <rect x="510" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="540" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#D97706">Irrational Numbers</text>
  <text x="540" y="585" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Non-terminating, non-repeating</text>
</svg>

learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the central idea and vocabulary.
slides.studentTakeaway: Understand the main concept and basic terms.
bulletPoints: The Number System in Class IX expands from Rational Numbers to include Irrational Numbers. Together, they form the set of Real Numbers.
onSlideText: The Number System in Class IX expands from Rational Numbers to include Irrational Numbers. Together, they form the set of Real Numbers.
speakerNotes: The Number System in Class IX expands from Rational Numbers to include Irrational Numbers. Together, they form the set of Real Numbers.

learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
slides.teacherIntent: Build the first core concept clearly.
slides.studentTakeaway: Grasp the first major idea of the lesson.
bulletPoints: Numbers expressible as p/q where p, q are integers and q ≠ 0.
Includes all terminating decimals (like 0.5) and recurring decimals (like 0.33...). Every integer is a rational number because it can be written over 1.
Terminating
Recurring
p/q form
onSlideText: Numbers expressible as p/q where p, q are integers and q ≠ 0.
Terminating
Recurring
p/q form
speakerNotes: Includes all terminating decimals (like 0.5) and recurring decimals (like 0.33...). Every integer is a rational number because it can be written over 1.
1/2 = 0.5 (Terminating)

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="960" height="720" viewBox="0 0 960 720" role="img" aria-label="Rational Numbers">
  <rect width="960" height="720" fill="#F8FBFD"/>
  <rect x="60" y="50" width="840" height="90" rx="24" fill="#1D4E89"/>
  <text x="90" y="106" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-weight="700" font-size="34" fill="#FFFFFF">Rational Numbers</text>
  <line x1="120" y1="390" x2="840" y2="390" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>

  <g font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937" text-anchor="middle">
    <line x1="180" y1="360" x2="180" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="180" y="450">-2</text>
    <line x1="300" y1="360" x2="300" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="300" y="450">-1</text>
    <line x1="420" y1="360" x2="420" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="420" y="450">0</text>
    <line x1="540" y1="360" x2="540" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="540" y="450">1</text>
    <line x1="660" y1="360" x2="660" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="660" y="450">2</text>
    <line x1="780" y1="360" x2="780" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="780" y="450">3</text>
  </g>
  <rect x="110" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#1D4E89" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="140" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#1D4E89">Rational Numbers</text>
  <text x="140" y="585" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Can be written as p/q</text>
  <rect x="510" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="540" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#D97706">Irrational Numbers</text>

</svg>

learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
slides.teacherIntent: Explain the second concept with visual emphasis.
slides.studentTakeaway: See the idea in a more visual form.
bulletPoints: Numbers that cannot be expressed as p/q. Their decimal expansion is non-terminating and non-recurring.
Examples include √2, √3, π. They fill the 'gaps' between rational numbers on the number line.
[object Object]
onSlideText: Non-terminating
Non-recurring
Geometric length
speakerNotes: Examples include √2, √3, π. They fill the 'gaps' between rational numbers on the number line.
[object Object]
Non-terminating
Non-recurring

    <line x1="300" y1="360" x2="300" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="300" y="450">-1</text>
    <line x1="420" y1="360" x2="420" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="420" y="450">0</text>
    <line x1="540" y1="360" x2="540" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="540" y="450">1</text>
    <line x1="660" y1="360" x2="660" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="660" y="450">2</text>
    <line x1="780" y1="360" x2="780" y2="420" stroke="#1F2937" stroke-width="4"/><text x="780" y="450">3</text>
  </g>
  <rect x="110" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#1D4E89" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="140" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#1D4E89">Rational Numbers</text>
  <text x="140" y="585" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="22" fill="#1F2937">Can be written as p/q</text>
  <rect x="510" y="500" width="340" height="120" rx="22" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#D97706" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="540" y="548" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#D97706">Irrational Numbers</text>

</svg>

learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
slides.teacherIntent: Model the thinking process step by step.
slides.studentTakeaway: See how the concept is applied.
speakerNotes: Teacher asks: 'Is 0 a rational number?' Students respond with reasoning (p/q form).
Teacher leads class through drawing perpendicular lines of length 1 unit.
Students calculate hypotenuse using Pythagoras theorem mentally.

learningOutcomeIds: Students can define rational and irrational numbers.
Students can distinguish between terminating, recurring, and non-recurring decimals.
bulletPoints: Number Line Construction Challenge
Draw a horizontal line representing the real number line.
Mark points O (0) and A (1).
At point A, draw a perpendicular line segment AB of length 1 unit upwards.
Join OB to form a right-angled triangle OAB.
onSlideText: Number Line Construction Challenge
Draw a horizontal line representing the real number line.
Mark points O (0) and A (1).
At point A, draw a perpendicular line segment AB of length 1 unit upwards.
speakerNotes: Draw a horizontal line representing the real number line.
Mark points O (0) and A (1).
At point A, draw a perpendicular line segment AB of length 1 unit upwards.
Join OB to form a right-angled triangle OAB.
Using a compass centered at O with radius equal to the length of hypotenuse OB, mark an arc on the number line. Label this intersection P.

pdf.documentTitle: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers - Student Revision Notes
keyInformation: We expanded our number system to include Irrational Numbers which fill the gaps between rational numbers.

docx.outlineTitle: Introduction to Number Systems: Rational vs Irrational Numbers - Teacher Lesson Outline
sections: This session introduces Class IX students to the expansion of the number system from rational numbers to include irrational numbers. Students will learn definitions, properties, and representations on the number line.
The Number System in Class IX expands from Rational Numbers to include Irrational Numbers. Together, they form the set of Real Numbers.
1. Ask students to write 5/3 on the board. Confirm it is rational.
2. Introduce the concept of non-terminating, non-recurring decimals using √2.
4. Demonstrate geometric construction for √2 on the number line step-by-step.
